tweets.full_text:
今朝もhttps://t.co/F7whNw1KeH に「Claude Code どう？」と聞いたら「優秀です」と返ってきました。 本人がそう言うなら間違いない？のでしょうか（笑） AI 初心者の私でも「自社プロダクトを褒める AI は正直、信用していい」のでしょうか？でも昨晩の"Windows で動かない"は AI にも分からなかった 。

結局、走らせてみないと分からないのは人間と同じで、行動あるのみだと再認識しました。私のモットーは走りながら考えようです。そのため、えらポンニュースも不備が多く気付いていらっしゃる方もいると思います。本当に心苦しいです。毎日、気付いたベースでメンテナンスをしているので皆様が今日の会議ネタで使いたいと思える読みやすい投稿記事を目指してブラッシュアップをしていきたいと思います。

tweets.full_text:
6:00 朝活開始

おはようございます！
めざましビクトリー開始
455日目の参加🔥

1週間前、僕は告白した。

「Udemy3作目が止まった。
犯人はClaude Codeでした」

あれから7日。
フォロワーは2,300人を超えた。

そして今、新しい誘惑がきた。
Xハーネス。
めちゃくちゃ気になる。

でも、今日は触らない。

同じ罠に2回ハマる人間にはならない。

「やりたい」が増えたら、
「やるべき」を1個に絞る。

今日はUdemy3作目だけ。
Xハーネスは、その後。

学んだことを、ちゃんと使う。

今日も一日楽しもう！
#めざましビクトリー

tweets.full_text:
Claude CAD (Claude Code hooked up to an MCP CAD viewer) uses scripted parametric CAD to build. Build123d and CadQuery are python open source libraries built around the OCCT kernel.

Scripting is fundamentally different from packaged parametric CAD. Patterning, conditionals, configurations are all native with scripted parametric CAD. In addition, you get github for diffs and versioning and anything else you could possibly want out of a PLM system. 

And, because Claude is very good at reasoning over code, it can manipulate these scripts with much more finesse than packaged CAD. Need to sweep over a parametric variable? Easy, just program it in. Check for collisions or interference? Just build it into the script. Morph geometry conditional to a particular parameter or integrate information from analysis? It’s just code. 

You can even build PLM approvals and release information into STEP files themselves, and the released STEP itself can be used as the source of truth. 

Moving from CAD models to drawings is still an issue, but given what I’ve seen of Solidworks and Creo drawing packages, I‘m sure a fairly lightweight solution can be built. Maybe it already has—I don’t think anyone is super-impressed by commercial CAD drawing packages. 

If drawings and curmudgeon-ism are the only things standing in the way of AI augmented CAD, I’m sure we can figure it out and free the ME field from packaged CAD lock-in.

tweets.full_text:
People are posting tons of GitHub repos for AI agents, Codex, Hermes, Claude Code, and other tools. Be careful what you download from the timeline.

Before installing anything, let your AI agent do a deep dive into the repo and generate a full report first:

✅ inspect commits and contributors

✅ scan dependencies and install scripts

✅ check for hidden network calls or obfuscated code

✅ review permissions and file access

✅ verify if the repo was copied and modified from another project

✅ look for wallet drainers, session stealers, clipboard hijackers, or API key collectors

Too many repos are built to steal crypto wallets, browser sessions, SSH keys, API keys, and sensitive files. One careless install can compromise your entire system.

In the AI era, your biggest edge is not moving fast blindly. It’s knowing what you can trust before you execute it.

tweets.full_text:
That @karpathy CLAUDE.md guidance going around is a good starting point.

But prose instructions are the weakest enforcement layer you can build.

I run Claude Code as my daily work environment for marketing strategy, competitive intel, content production — not code.

There are layers beyond prose, and each one catches what the last one can't:

Prose rules — You write "think before acting" in your CLAUDE.md and the model reads it. Sometimes follows it, sometimes doesn't. You find out when the output is wrong.

Hooks — A rule that intercepts the behavior before it happens. I kept re-explaining "don't spawn Explore subagents" until I wrote a hook that blocks it at the system level. The model doesn't decide whether to comply — it can't.

Skills — Codified workflows with enforced sequence. My editing pipeline runs de-LLMification → clarity → voice validation in that order, every time, because the skill defines it.

Agent architecture — Specialized agents with scoped tools. My competitive intelligence agent has web scraping access. My editing agents don't. You can't touch what your tools don't reach.

Success criteria — This is Karpathy's best insight, buried in his post. Don't tell the model what to do — tell it what done looks like. "Complete when it has 3 sourced alternatives with evidence tiers and data within 90 days." The model loops until it gets there.

Start with the CLAUDE.md. Then look at which rules you keep re-explaining — those are your first hooks. The layers build from there.
---

Three graphics showing my real applications:
1. Strategic Theater — "don't use strategic theater language"
- Bad: "compelling strategic opportunity with transformative potential"
- Good: "$2.4B market, growing 18% YoY, 3.2% penetration"

2. Evidence Tiers — "don't present speculation as fact"
- Bad: "Our analysis shows this market could reach $8B by 2028"
- Good: "Gartner estimates $8B (Projected). We've measured $1.2B in our segment."

3. Context First — "check what we already know before responding"
- Bad: "Let me create a new competitive analysis from scratch"
- Good: "Found 3 prior analyses in vault. Building on the March assessment."

quoted_status.full_text:
Andrej Karpathy wrote something that every Claude Code user has felt but couldn't articulate.

Three quotes. Read them slowly.

"The models make wrong assumptions on your behalf and just run along with them without checking. They don't manage their confusion, don't seek clarifications, don't surface inconsistencies, don't present tradeoffs, don't push back when they should."

"They really like to overcomplicate code and APIs, bloat abstractions, don't clean up dead code... implement a bloated construction over 1000 lines when 100 would do."

"They still sometimes change/remove comments and code they don't sufficiently understand as side effects, even if orthogonal to the task."

You've seen all three. Probably this week.

Someone turned these three observations into a single CLAUDE[.]md file. Four principles, one install, directly addresses each quote:

1./ Think before coding
Don't assume. Don't hide confusion. State ambiguity explicitly. Present multiple interpretations rather than silently picking one. Push back if a simpler approach exists. Stop and ask rather than guess.

2./ Simplicity first
No features beyond what was asked. No abstractions for single-use code. No "flexibility" that wasn't requested. No error handling for impossible scenarios. The test: would a senior engineer say this is overcomplicated? If yes, rewrite it.

3./ Surgical changes
Don't "improve" adjacent code. Don't refactor things that aren't broken. Match the existing style even if you'd do it differently. If you notice unrelated dead code, mention it, don't delete it. Every changed line should trace directly to the request.

4./ Goal-driven execution
Transform "fix the bug" into "write a test that reproduces it, then make it pass." Transform "add validation" into "write tests for invalid inputs, then make them pass." Give it success criteria and watch it loop until done.

This last one is Karpathy's key insight captured directly: "LLMs are exceptionally good at looping until they meet specific goals... Don't tell it what to do, give it success criteria and watch it go."

It's a single file. Drop it into any project.

tweets.full_text:
MCP SERVERS ARE THE MOST POWERFUL FEATURE IN CLAUDE CODE AND MOST PEOPLE HAVE NO IDEA HOW THEY WORK.

Here is the complete beginner breakdown.

Three things make Claude Code powerful.

Skills give Claude instructions for how to do specific tasks.

Hooks act as guardrails that keep Claude operating within defined boundaries.

MCP servers give Claude entirely new functional abilities it did not have before.

That third category is the one that changes everything.

Without MCP: Claude can read and write code.

With MCP: Claude can browse the web, manage databases, take screenshots, interact with external applications, and operate across your entire tool stack autonomously.

The setup takes 5 minutes.

Create or edit an .mcp.json file in your workspace. Add the servers you want. Restart Claude Code.

Every MCP server you add is a new capability Claude did not have before you added it.

Find available servers at https://t.co/lVmF8UWquY. It is the central repository for MCP plugins and it is growing every week.

Here are two workflows that show what this actually enables.

Browser automation: Connect the Chrome DevTools MCP. Claude now takes bulk screenshots of any websites you specify for design inspiration, competitive analysis, or visual reference. No manual browsing.

Database management: Connect Supabase MCP. Claude transforms document SOPs into searchable vector databases automatically. Your entire knowledge base becomes queryable in natural language.

THE WARNING NOBODY TELLS YOU.

Every MCP server consumes tokens from your context window.

Add 15 to 20 servers and you will exhaust your context budget before Claude finishes a complex task.

The best practice: add only the servers your current workflow actually needs. Once a workflow is tested and stable convert it into a Skill. Skills accomplish the same result at a fraction of the token cost.

Right tool. Right time. Right token budget.

Bookmark this before you add your first MCP server.

Follow @cyrilXBT for every Claude Code workflow that makes the tool more powerful without breaking your budget.

tweets.full_text:
A NON-DESIGNER CAN NOW SHIP UI THAT LOOKS LIKE IT WAS BUILT BY A $200,000-A-YEAR PRODUCT DESIGNER USING 3 FREE CLAUDE CODE SKILLS YOU CAN INSTALL IN UNDER 60 SECONDS.

The 3 skills that change everything:

Emil Kowalski Design Skill. Emil is one of the most respected UI designers on the internet. He spent 10 years developing motion design taste. He just encoded all of it into a skill file. Install it and Claude adds proper easing curves, micro-interactions, and animations that feel intentional. Your UI stops looking flat and dead.

Impeccable Design. 20 commands covering typography, color, spacing, and layout. The one you'll use most is /polish. Run it before shipping and Claude tightens every element in your interface. It's the design equivalent of what linters do for code.

Taste Skill. This one is the killer. It pulls real design references and teaches Claude what good design actually looks like. No more generic gradients. No more Inter font on everything. No more layouts that scream Tailwind starter template. Output looks like a human designer actually touched it.

Here's the math nobody is doing.

A freelance designer charges $5,000 to $15,000 for a landing page in 2026. A Webflow developer charges $3,000 to $8,000 for the same output. An agency starts at $25,000 with a 6-week timeline and 4 stakeholder calls before they show you anything.

Same quality output through Claude Code with these 3 skills: 90 minutes of work.

I'm not exaggerating. The before-and-after comparisons are insane.

Before these skills, AI-generated UI had a tell. Generic gradients. Default Inter font. Flat layouts. Zero personality. A trained designer could spot AI output in 3 seconds.

After these skills, that tell disappears.

A 17-year-old shipping his first SaaS from his bedroom can produce UI that looks indistinguishable from the work of a senior designer at Stripe. Same model. Same prompts. Different skill stack. Wildly different outcome.

The window for charging $10,000 for a landing page is closing fast.

The window for charging $3,000 by knowing which 3 skills to install is wide open right now.

Install. Ship. Charge. Repeat.

This is the cleanest asymmetric opportunity in the AI design space right now and most people will scroll past this post without acting on it.

Don't be most people.
